justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO-monitoring gap — called out as CRITICAL in metrics gaps and in the first component interaction. (2) Retry tracking gap clearly identified — retry_queue interaction marked 'broken', retry SLO called 'completely unmonitored'. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — explicitly states existing metrics measure 'API-level symptoms, not business-level outcomes' and that the error-count alert cannot answer SLO compliance questions. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds, renewal_retry_attempts_total, renewal_queue_depth gauge, slo_renewal_on_time_ratio, retry_exhausted_total, retry_pending_total. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — burn-rate monitoring dashboard and SLO breach alerting rules explicitly called out in infra_provisioning. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with failure mode analysis, circuit breaker gaps, DLQ recommendations, and poison message handling
Strong framing of the core problem: 'You cannot measure what you claim to guarantee' — precisely the insight this scenario tests for
Correctly distinguishes between log coverage (adequate for forensics) and metric coverage (inadequate for real-time SLO alerting)
On-call engineer perspective is effective — concretely lists questions that cannot be answered with current instrumentation
Escalations are well-structured and correctly routed, with clear reasoning
Could have been more explicit about burn-rate alerting mechanics (e.g., multi-window burn rate à la Google SRE) rather than just naming it in infra_provisioning
